title:
High-Level Design: Design Video's View Count Service for YouTube/Netflix/Instagram
MEDIUM
role
Senior Software Engineer

content.text:
High-Level Design: Design Video's View Count Service for YouTube/Netflix/Instagram
MEDIUM
role
Senior Software Engineer
Round 3
High-Level Design (HLD)
System Design (HLD)
exp
#287 · Apr 2025
● offer
High Scalability
High Availability
High-Level Design (HLD)
System Design
Backend

▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Design a system which can give total, monthly weekly views on a video to show on creator's dashboard.

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — counting accuracy, the write firehose, and the time-window rollups — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension here: **a massive, bursty write firehose of view events** (hundreds of thousands per second) versus a **creator dashboard that only needs approximate, time-windowed totals read at human speed**. We can trade exactness and freshness for throughput — and that trade is the whole design.

🏢 I'll target a **Meta/YouTube-style bar** — front-load scale and the write hot-path. Tell me if it's Amazon (I'll make cost/ops first-class) and I'll re-tilt.

**Functional (core flows — above the line):**
- Users (viewers) generate a **view event** when they watch a video — the system must ingest and count it.
- Creators should be able to see **total views** on a video.
- Creators should be able to see **windowed views** — last 7 days (weekly) and last 30 days (monthly), per video, on their dashboard.

**Below the line (out of scope):**
- Defining *what counts as a view* (30s watched? Netflix 2-min rule?) — I'll treat "view" as a validated event upstream.
- Per-viewer analytics, demographics, watch-time, retention curves.
- Real-time sub-second counters shown to viewers ("1.2M views" under the player) — I'll mention it but the *creator dashboard* is the ask.
- Monetization / ad counting (needs exactly-once, different system).

**Non-Functional (talkable targets):**
- **Scale:** ~500K view events/sec peak ingest; billions of videos; dashboard reads ~thousands/sec.
- **Availability:** 4 nines (99.99%) on the read dashboard; ingest must never drop on the floor — degrade to buffering, not data loss.
- **Latency:** dashboard p99 < ~300ms; ingest accept p99 < ~50ms (fire-and-forget).
- **Consistency:** **eventual** is fine — counts can lag seconds-to-a-minute. Approximate counts (±0.1%) are acceptable for the dashboard; I am NOT building exactly-once accounting here.
- **Durability:** an accepted event should survive a node crash (RPO near zero via a durable log), but a few dropped events under catastrophe is tolerable for *view counts* (not for billing).

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** true exactly-once counting is expensive and unnecessary for view counts; I'll deliberately choose at-least-once + idempotent dedup and accept a tiny error bound. If this were ad-billing I'd redesign.

> 💬 "Before I draw anything — the key insight is the read and write profiles are wildly different: a firehose in, gentle dashboard queries out. So I'll optimize the two paths separately."

🎙️ **Script:** "So this is a view-count service feeding a creator dashboard — total, weekly, and monthly views per video. The thing that jumps out at me is the asymmetry: we're ingesting maybe half a million view events a second, but the dashboard is read by creators a few thousand times a second and they're happy with numbers that are a few seconds stale and approximately right. That means I'll treat counts as eventually consistent and approximate, optimize the write path for raw throughput with a durable buffer so we never lose accepted events, and pre-aggregate on the read side so the dashboard is just a cheap key lookup. I'm explicitly not building exactly-once billing-grade accounting — I'll call that out as the line I'm not crossing."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each one moves the design:

- **"Is approximate good enough, or do these counts drive payments?"** — This is the biggest fork. Approximate → counters/sketches and we're done. Exact → exactly-once pipeline, dedup at scale, much more cost. *I'll assume approximate.*
- **"What windows exactly — rolling 7/30 days, or calendar week/month?"** — Rolling windows need time-bucketed counts I can sum; calendar windows are simpler. *I'll assume rolling 7-day and 30-day, plus all-time total.*
- **"How fresh must the dashboard be?"** — Sub-second changes the architecture vs. "within a minute is fine." *I'll assume ~1 minute freshness is acceptable.*
- **"Do we need per-region breakdowns or one global number?"** — Multi-region rollup adds a merge step. *I'll assume one global count per video, but ingest is multi-region.*
- **"What's the dedup requirement — does a viewer refreshing count once or many times?"** — Defines idempotency key. *I'll assume each validated view event arrives with a unique event_id and we dedup on it (at-least-once delivery).*
- **"Retention of raw events?"** — Drives storage. *I'll assume raw events kept ~90 days in cheap storage, aggregates kept forever.*

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — approximate counts, rolling 7/30-day windows, ~1-minute freshness, dedup on a client event_id. Are you happy with me treating this as approximate and eventually consistent, or do you want me to design the exact/billing version instead?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll defer full estimation and just compute the few numbers that actually drive decisions: the write QPS, the durable-log throughput, and the aggregate storage.

Let me compute these exactly.

input.content:
# Assumptions
peak_views_per_sec = 500_000          # peak ingest
avg_views_per_sec  = 150_000          # average
event_bytes        = 200              # video_id, event_id, ts, region, device

# Write firehose
print("Peak ingest QPS:", peak_views_per_sec)
print("Avg ingest QPS :", avg_views_per_sec)

# Durable log bandwidth at peak
log_bw_mb = peak_views_per_sec * event_bytes / 1e6
print(f"Peak log write bandwidth: {log_bw_mb:.0f} MB/s")

# Daily event volume
daily = avg_views_per_sec * 86400
print(f"Daily events: {daily/1e9:.1f} billion/day")

# Raw event storage for 90 days, replication factor 3
raw_bytes = daily * 90 * event_bytes * 3
print(f"Raw events 90d x3 replicas: {raw_bytes/1e12:.0f} TB")

# Aggregate store: per-video time buckets.
# Say 2 billion videos, but only ~50M get views in any given hour.
# All-time + hourly buckets for 30 days (720 buckets) per active video.
videos_total = 2_000_000_000
agg_row_bytes = 40   # video_id + bucket + count
# all-time counter per video:
alltime = videos_total * agg_row_bytes * 3
print(f"All-time counters (2B videos) x3: {alltime/1e12:.1f} TB")

# hourly buckets: assume 200M videos viewed in trailing 30d, 720 buckets
hourly = 200_000_000 * 720 * agg_row_bytes * 3
print(f"Hourly buckets (200M active x720) x3: {hourly/1e12:.0f} TB")

# Redis hot counter ceiling check
redis_node = 150_000   # ops/sec realistic
print(f"Redis nodes needed for peak if all hit one tier: {peak_views_per_sec/redis_node:.1f}")

print(f"All-time counters (2B videos) x3: {alltime/1e12:.1f} TB")

content.text:
| Metric | Value | Why it matters | |---|---|---| | Peak ingest | ~500K events/s | The binding constraint — drives the durable log + partitioning | | Avg ingest | ~150K events/s | Sizes steady-state aggregation workers | | Log write bandwidth | ~100 MB/s | Trivial for Kafka — buffering is not the bottleneck, processing is | | Daily events | ~13 billion/day | Sizes raw retention | | Raw events (90d, x3) | ~700 TB | Cheap object/columnar storage, not the primary DB | | All-time counters (2B videos, x3) | ~0.2 TB | Tiny — fits a KV store easily | | Hourly buckets (200M active x720, x3) | ~17 TB | The real aggregate store; drives the window queries | | Redis nodes for peak | ~3-4 | Only if every event hit one counter tier | **The ONE number that forces a decision:** ~500K writes/sec. **Flip threshold:** a single Redis node tops out ~150K ops/s and a single Kafka partition ~tens of MB/s — so we **cannot** have one counter or one partition; we **must** shard the ingest and pre-aggregate, never letting raw event QPS hit the database. Glossary as I go: **QPS** *(queries per second — how many requests hit the system each second)*. **Kafka** *(a durable append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a downstream consumer is slow — here it absorbs the 500K/s spike)*. **TTL** *(time-to-live — auto-expiry on cached data)*. 🎙️ **Script:** "Let me get the scale on the board. We're looking at about half a million view events a second at peak, versus a dashboard that's read maybe a few thousand times a second — so it's a write-heavy, read-light system with a huge asymmetry. That firehose is only about 100 megabytes a second of raw bytes, so *buffering* it is easy — Kafka eats that for breakfast. The hard part is that no single counter can take 500K writes a second, so the whole game is sharding the ingest and pre-aggregating into time buckets, so by the time a creator loads their dashboard it's just a cheap key lookup. Storage-wise the aggregates are small — a few terabytes — and the 700 terabytes of raw events go to cheap columnar storage we rarely touch." ## 4. Core Entities First draft of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later: - **View Event** — a single validated watch: which video, a unique event_id (for dedup), timestamp, region, device. - **Video** — the thing being counted; identified by video_id (our partition key everywhere). - **Counter / Aggregate** — the pre-computed numbers: all-time total, and per-time-bucket counts that we sum into 7-day and 30-day windows. - **Creator** — owns videos; the dashboard caller we authorize against. 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are simple: a view event coming in, the video it belongs to, the creator who owns it, and the aggregates — the all-time counter plus time-bucketed counts we roll up into weekly and monthly windows. This is just a first pass; I'll nail down the exact fields when I get to the data model." ## 5. API / Interface Going one-by-one through the functional requirements: **Ingest a view (FR1):** ``` POST /v1/views Body: { video_id, event_id, ts, region, device } Resp: 202 Accepted ``` Why: POST creates an event; **202** (not 200) because we accept-and-buffer — processing is async/fire-and-forget so ingest p99 stays under ~50ms. `event_id` is the **idempotency key** — a retried client send dedups to the same logical view. The view's *validity* (did they watch long enough) is decided upstream; we trust the event. **Get total + windows for a video (FR2, FR3):** ``` GET /v1/videos/{video_id}/views?windows=total,7d,30d Resp: 200 { video_id, total, "7d": ..., "30d": ..., as_of_ts } ``` Why: GET reads; one endpoint returns all windows so the dashboard makes one call. `as_of_ts` tells the creator how fresh the number is (honest about eventual consistency). **Get all of a creator's videos (dashboard load):** ``` GET /v1/creators/me/videos/views?windows=total,7d,30d Resp: 200 [ {video_id, total, 7d, 30d}, ... ] ``` Why: dashboard shows many videos; batch read avoids N calls. **Security:** caller identity comes from the **JWT** *(a signed token proving who you are, so we never trust a client-supplied creator_id)*. We authorize that the creator owns the requested videos before returning counts. Ingest is rate-limited per-IP/per-device to blunt bot inflation. 🎙️ **Script:** "Three endpoints. Ingest is a POST that returns 202 Accepted — we buffer and process async, and the client-supplied event_id is our idempotency key so retries don't double-count. Reads are GETs: one returns total, 7-day and 30-day for a single video in one shot with an as-of timestamp so we're honest about freshness, and a batch one returns every video for the logged-in creator's dashboard. Identity always comes from the JWT — I never trust a client-sent creator_id." ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) ### 6.1 "Ingest and count a view event" Start minimal: accept events without dropping them, and don't let the firehose touch a database directly. New components: a stateless **Ingest API**, a **Kafka** durable log partitioned by video_id, and **aggregation workers** that consume and increment counters in a **counter store**. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients P[Player clients] end subgraph Ingest_write_path LB[Load balancer] ING[Ingest API stateless] K[[Kafka log
partitioned by video_id]] AGG[Aggregation workers] CS[(Counter store
sharded KV)] end P -->|1 POST view 202| LB LB -->|2 forward| ING ING -->|3 append event| K K -->|4 consume batches| AGG AGG -->|5 dedup + increment| CS classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class LB,ING,K,AGG,CS cold; ``` 1. Player fires a view event; **Load balancer** routes to any **Ingest API** node. 2. LB forwards to a stateless ingest node (scales horizontally — no state to coordinate). 3. Ingest validates shape, then **appends the event to Kafka**, keyed by video_id so all events for a video land on one partition (ordering + locality). Returns 202 immediately. *State change: event now durably buffered.* 4. **Aggregation workers** consume Kafka in batches (e.g., flush every 1s or 10K events). 5. Each worker **dedups on event_id** (recent-window set) and **increments the counter store**. *State change: counts updated.* > 💬 "I'm deliberately putting Kafka between ingest and the database. Ingest just appends and returns — so a database slowdown can never reject a viewer's event; it just queues up." ### 6.2 "Show total views" + 6.3 "Show weekly/monthly views" Now the read side. The trick: workers don't keep just one number — they increment **per-time-bucket counters** (e.g., hourly buckets) AND an all-time total. Windows = sum of buckets. New components: a **Read API**, a **Redis cache** for hot dashboard reads, and the **bucketed aggregate store**. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients P[Player clients] D[Creator dashboard] end subgraph Write_path LB[Load balancer] ING[Ingest API] K[[Kafka log]] AGG[Aggregation workers] end subgraph Read_path RAPI[Read API] RED[Redis cache] CS[(Aggregate store
all-time + hourly buckets
sharded by video_id)] end P -->|1 POST view| LB --> ING -->|2 append| K K -->|3 consume| AGG -->|4 increment bucket + total| CS D -->|5 GET views| RAPI RAPI -->|6 check cache| RED RED -->|7 miss| CS RAPI -->|8 sum buckets to 7d 30d| D classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class LB,ING,K,AGG,CS cold; class RAPI,RED,D hot; ``` 5. Creator dashboard calls **Read API**. 6. Read API checks **Redis** *(in-memory cache — sub-ms lookups, holds recently-viewed videos' counts with a ~1-min TTL)*. 7. On miss, read the all-time counter and the relevant hourly buckets from the **aggregate store**. 8. **Sum the trailing 168 hourly buckets for 7d, 720 for 30d**, return with as_of_ts; populate Redis. > 💬 "Weekly and monthly aren't separate counters — they're just sums over hourly buckets. That's the key idea: store fine-grained buckets once, derive any window by summing." **Final (high-level):** the cumulative diagram above is the whole system — write path (amber) feeds the aggregate store; read path (green) serves the dashboard from cache + bucket sums. Load-bearing schema: aggregate store keyed by `(video_id, bucket_hour)` plus a `(video_id, total)` row. 🎙️ **Script:** "End to end: a view comes in, the ingest API appends it to Kafka keyed by video_id and returns immediately. Aggregation workers consume in batches, dedup on event_id, and bump two things — the all-time total and the current hour's bucket. On the read side, the dashboard hits the read API, which serves from Redis if it's warm, and on a miss reads the all-time counter plus the trailing hourly buckets and sums 168 of them for the week and 720 for the month. The big idea is I store fine-grained hourly buckets once and derive every window by summing — I never keep a separate 'weekly counter' that I'd have to expire." 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The two juiciest parts are how I make counting accurate-enough at 500K/s without a hot-shard meltdown, and how I do the windowed rollups efficiently. Which do you want first — or is there something else you'd probe?" ## 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Key fields | Store + why | |---|---|---| | **View Event (raw)** | event_id, video_id, ts, region, device | **Kafka** (transient buffer) → **columnar object store** (S3/Parquet) for 90d. Append-only, rarely read, cheap. | | **All-time counter** | video_id (PK), count | **Sharded KV** (e.g., Cassandra/ScyllaDB or Redis-backed) — point read/write by video_id. | | **Hourly bucket** | (video_id, bucket_hour) PK, count | **Wide-column store** (Cassandra) — partition by video_id, clustered by bucket_hour → range-scan trailing N hours fast. | | **Dedup set** | event_id → seen (TTL ~10 min) | **Redis** with TTL — short-window idempotency. | | **Video → creator** | video_id, creator_id | **SQL** (metadata, low volume) for ownership authz. | **Partition/shard key is video_id everywhere** — co-locates all of a video's events and counts, makes window scans a single-partition range read. **Consistency:** counters are eventually consistent (last-writer + atomic increment); ownership lookup is strongly consistent (it's authz). **Cassandra** *(a wide-column NoSQL store that shards by partition key and handles massive write throughput with tunable consistency — here it holds the bucketed counts because we need fast writes and partition-local range scans)*. 🎙️ **Script:** "Everything is keyed by video_id so a video's events and counts live together. Raw events go to Kafka then cheap columnar storage for 90 days. The counts live in a wide-column store like Cassandra — all-time as a single counter row, and hourly buckets clustered by hour so reading a 30-day window is one partition-local range scan of 720 small rows. Dedup uses a short-TTL Redis set on event_id. The only strongly-consistent thing is the video-to-creator ownership lookup, because that's authorization." ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to "firehose in, gentle reads out" and ask which path the bottleneck is on — that re-derives every answer here. ### How do we count 500K events/sec without a hot shard melting down? #### Bad: Increment a row in a SQL DB per event **Approach:** each view does `UPDATE counts SET c=c+1 WHERE video_id=?`. **Challenges:** 500K writes/sec of row-locked updates is impossible on one row; a viral video sends every write to ONE row → lock contention, the **hot-shard** problem. ⚠️ **Trap:** candidates who write straight to the DB per event fail here — you must decouple and batch. #### Good: Kafka buffer + batched increments **Approach:** append to Kafka (keyed by video_id), workers consume in micro-batches and do one increment per video per batch (combine 10K events into "+10K"). **Challenges:** dramatically cuts DB write QPS, but a **celebrity video** still pins all its events to one Kafka partition and one worker → single-partition bottleneck. #### Great: Kafka + in-memory pre-aggregation + hot-key splitting **Approach:** Workers keep an in-memory map and flush aggregated deltas every ~1s (so the DB sees ~one write per active video per second, not per event). For **hot keys**, split the counter into N sub-counters: write to `video_id#shard_k` (k = random 0..N-1), and **sum the N shards on read**. This spreads a celebrity's writes across N partitions/rows. ```mermaid flowchart TD K[[Kafka by video_id]] -->|consume batch| W[Worker in-memory aggregate] W -->|flush delta 1s| SUB[(Sub-counters
video_id#0..N)] R[Read API] -->|sum N shards| SUB classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class K,W,SUB cold; class R hot; ``` **Math:** without batching, 500K writes/s hits the DB. Batching 10K events into 1 write per video per second drops DB writes to roughly the number of *active videos/sec* — say ~50K/s spread across shards → comfortably ~10K/s per Cassandra node. Hot-key splitting with N=10 keeps any single celebrity under a node's ~150K/s ceiling. 🎙️ **Script:** "The naive thing — incrementing a DB row per view — dies instantly because a viral video hammers one row. So I buffer everything in Kafka and have workers aggregate in memory and flush a combined delta about once a second, which turns 500K writes a second into one write per active video. For the celebrity case where even one video's stream is too hot, I split its counter into ten sub-counters with a random suffix and sum them on read — that fans the writes across ten partitions so no single row is a bottleneck." 🧠 **If they ask "why not just one counter per video?":** "Because a single viral video's writes all serialize on one row or partition — the hot-shard problem. Splitting into N sub-counters trades a slightly more expensive read, summing N rows, for write parallelism, which is the right trade since writes are the firehose." ### How do we compute rolling 7-day and 30-day windows efficiently? #### Bad: Scan raw events at read time **Approach:** on dashboard load, count events in the last 7/30 days from raw storage. **Challenges:** scanning billions of rows per dashboard load — seconds-to-minutes latency, blows the 300ms SLO. #### Great: Pre-bucketed time-series counters **Approach:** workers increment a **per-hour bucket** `(video_id, hour)`. A 7-day window = sum of trailing 168 hourly buckets; 30-day = 720. Range-scan is partition-local and tiny. Old buckets expire via TTL (keep ~35 days). The all-time total is its own monotonic counter (never expires). ```mermaid flowchart LR E[Event ts] -->|floor to hour| B[(video_id, hour bucket)] Q[7d query] -->|sum last 168| B Q2[30d query] -->|sum last 720| B classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class Q,Q2 hot; ``` **Trade-off:** hourly granularity means "last 7 days" is accurate to the hour, not the second — totally fine for a dashboard. If they want minute precision, smaller buckets cost ~60x storage. Pre-compute daily rollups to make 30d even cheaper (sum 30 daily + a few hourly for the current day). 🎙️ **Script:** "I never scan raw events at read time — that's death. Instead workers write per-hour buckets, and a weekly number is just the sum of the last 168 hourly buckets, monthly is 720. It's a tiny partition-local range scan. Buckets expire after about 35 days, and the all-time total is a separate counter that never expires. The cost is hour-level precision on the windows, which is completely fine for a creator dashboard." 🧠 **If they ask "why hourly, not store every window?":** "Storing fine buckets once and summing lets me serve *any* window — 7d, 30d, even custom ranges — without maintaining and expiring separate weekly/monthly counters that drift. One source of truth." ### How do we avoid double-counting (idempotency)? #### Great: at-least-once delivery + event_id dedup **Approach:** Kafka gives at-least-once, so retries/redelivery can duplicate. Each event carries a client-generated **event_id**; workers check a Redis dedup set (TTL ~10 min, covering the redelivery window) before incrementing. Within a worker's batch, dedup in-memory too. **Trade-off:** the TTL window bounds dedup — a duplicate arriving hours later (rare) slips through, causing a tiny over-count. Acceptable given ±0.1% tolerance. For exact counting we'd need a persistent dedup store keyed by event_id, much costlier. 🎙️ **Script:** "Kafka is at-least-once so I have to dedup. Every event has a unique event_id and workers check a short-TTL Redis set before counting it. The TTL covers the realistic redelivery window; a duplicate arriving days later would slip through, but that's a fraction of a percent and within tolerance. If this were billing I'd use a persistent dedup store and eat the cost." 🧠 **If they ask "what about bot/fraud inflation?":** "That's upstream view-validation's job — rate limiting, bot detection, watch-time thresholds decide what's a valid event before it reaches me. I just count validated events idempotently." ### How do we approximate cheaply when even counters are too much? (optional/scale) For *unique* viewer counts (not in scope but a likely probe) I'd use **HyperLogLog** *(a probabilistic sketch that estimates distinct counts in ~12KB with ~2% error instead of storing every viewer id)* — Redis supports it natively. For total views, exact counters are cheap enough so I keep them. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost - **Availability:** read path 4 nines via multi-AZ Redis + Cassandra replicas; ingest path stays up as long as Kafka does (replicated 3x). - **Per-dependency degradation:** - *Cassandra slow/down:* ingest unaffected (Kafka buffers); reads serve stale Redis values with an older as_of_ts. Workers replay from Kafka offset when DB recovers — no data loss. - *Redis down:* reads fall back to direct Cassandra (higher latency, still correct); dedup falls back to in-batch only (tiny over-count risk). - *Worker crash:* Kafka consumer group rebalances; offsets are committed only after DB flush → at-least-once, no loss. - *Kafka down:* ingest API buffers locally briefly then 503s — the only true degradation; mitigated by 3x replication + multi-broker. - **RPO/RTO:** Kafka RPO≈0 (replicated). Aggregate store RPO ~minutes (replayable from Kafka/raw events — counts are *reconstructable*, a big advantage). RTO minutes via replica failover. **Tested restore:** periodically rebuild a sample video's counts from raw events and reconcile against live counters (drift alarm). - **Multi-region:** ingest regionally, each region writes local sub-counters; a rollup job merges per-region counts into the global number (eventually consistent). Single global write region not needed since increments are commutative. - **Cost (rough monthly):** dominant driver is **Kafka + worker compute** for the firehose and **~700TB raw storage** (cheap on object storage, ~$15K/mo). Aggregate store is small. Ballpark mid-five-figures/month; raw event storage and Kafka throughput dominate, not the dashboard reads. - **Observability:** SLO burn-rate alerts on ingest accept rate and read p99; consumer lag alert on Kafka (the leading indicator of staleness); a daily reconciliation job comparing summed buckets vs raw counts. 🎙️ **Script:** "The beautiful property here is counts are reconstructable — if the aggregate store gets corrupted I replay Kafka or the raw events and rebuild them, so my real RPO is near zero. The only hard-down failure is Kafka itself, which I replicate three ways. Everything else degrades gracefully: DB slow just means staler dashboard numbers, Redis down just means slower reads. My number-one health metric is Kafka consumer lag, because that's exactly how stale the dashboard is." ## 10. Trade-off Ledger - **Approximate (±0.1%) vs exact counts** — I gave up exactness (TTL-bounded dedup, hot-key shard summing) to hit 500K/s cheaply. *Reverses if* counts drive payments → persistent dedup + exactly-once → far higher cost. - **Hourly bucket granularity** — gave up sub-hour window precision for ~60x storage savings. *Reverses if* product needs minute-level "views in last hour" trends. - **Eventual consistency / ~1-min freshness** — gave up read-your-own-writes immediacy (a creator's brand-new view may not show for up to a minute due to Kafka lag + flush interval). *Reverses if* sub-second live counts are required → shorter flush + dual-write to a live counter, more load. 🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'd flag: I chose approximate over exact, hourly buckets over finer granularity, and eventual over strong consistency. All three trade accuracy or freshness for throughput and cost, which is right for a creator dashboard. Every one of them flips if the requirement changes to billing-grade or real-time — and I'd redesign the dedup and flush paths specifically." ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **1. What if a video goes viral — the hot-key problem?** All its events hash to one partition/row and overwhelm it. I split the counter into N sub-counters with a random suffix per write and sum on read, spreading writes across N partitions. I can even detect hotness dynamically and raise N for that video. The cost is an N-way read, negligible since reads are rare. > 💬 "I shard the hot video's counter into N sub-counters and sum them on read — writes fan out, reads pay a tiny tax." **2. How do you prevent double-counting on Kafka redelivery?** Each event has a unique event_id; workers dedup against a short-TTL Redis set before incrementing, plus in-batch dedup. Offsets commit only after a successful DB flush, so it's at-least-once with idempotent application. The TTL bounds the dedup window, allowing a negligible over-count for very-late duplicates. > 💬 "At-least-once delivery plus event_id dedup makes the increment effectively idempotent within the redelivery window." **3. How fresh is the dashboard, and why isn't it real-time?** Freshness equals Kafka consumer lag plus the worker flush interval — about a minute normally. I trade immediacy for batching efficiency. If sub-second is required, I'd shorten the flush and keep a separate live counter, accepting more DB load. > 💬 "Freshness is basically consumer lag plus flush interval — about a minute, tunable down at a cost." **4. How do you compute rolling windows without scanning raw events?** I pre-aggregate into hourly buckets keyed by (video_id, hour); 7-day is the sum of 168 buckets, 30-day is 720, a tiny partition-local range scan. Daily rollups make 30d cheaper still. Old buckets TTL out after ~35 days. > 💬 "Windows are sums of pre-computed hourly buckets — never a raw scan." **5. What happens when the aggregate DB goes down?** Ingest is unaffected because Kafka keeps buffering; reads serve stale cached values with an older as_of_ts. When the DB recovers, workers resume from committed offsets and catch up. Because counts are reconstructable from Kafka/raw events, there's effectively no permanent loss. > 💬 "DB down just means staler reads — ingest keeps buffering and we replay on recovery." **6. How do you handle multi-region ingest?** Each region counts locally into region-scoped sub-counters, and a rollup merges them into the global number. Since increments are commutative and we want eventual consistency, no single global write region is needed. Cross-region merge adds seconds of lag, fine for the dashboard. > 💬 "Regions count locally and we merge — increments commute, so no global coordinator needed." **7. Is there any single global coordinator / bottleneck?** No central sequencer — that's deliberate. The closest is a per-video counter, which I shard for hot keys. Kafka partitions and Cassandra shards both scale horizontally by video_id, so there's no single point that caps throughput. > 💬 "I avoided any global counter or sequencer — everything shards by video_id." **8. How do you stop bots/fraud inflating counts?** View *validity* is decided upstream — rate limiting, device fingerprinting, watch-time thresholds — before events reach me. I only count validated events and dedup them. Keeping fraud logic upstream keeps my pipeline a pure, fast counter. > 💬 "Fraud filtering is upstream; I just idempotently count what's already validated." **9. Why Kafka and not just write to the DB or use a queue like SQS?** Kafka gives a durable, replayable, partitioned log — replay is what makes counts reconstructable and lets me rebuild state after failures. A plain queue loses messages once consumed; the DB can't absorb 500K/s of row updates. The log is the backbone of both durability and recovery. > 💬 "Kafka's replayability is what gives me near-zero RPO and rebuildable counters." **10. How do you size and scale the workers?** Workers are a Kafka consumer group; partition count sets max parallelism, and I scale workers up to the partition count. I monitor consumer lag as the scaling signal — lag rising means add workers or partitions. Each worker does cheap in-memory aggregation, so a handful per region handles 150K/s average. > 💬 "Workers scale with Kafka partitions; consumer lag is my autoscaling trigger." **11. What's the storage cost driver?** Not the counters — those are a couple terabytes. It's the ~700TB of 90-day raw events, which I push to cheap columnar object storage, and Kafka retention. I keep raw events mainly for reconciliation and reprocessing, and could shorten retention to cut cost. > 💬 "Counters are tiny; raw event retention dominates storage, so it lives on cheap object storage." **12. How would you extend this to per-country or per-device breakdowns later?** Add region/device to the bucket key — `(video_id, hour, region)` — and sum the appropriate subset. Storage grows by the cardinality of the new dimension, so I'd only add dimensions the product needs and pre-aggregate the common rollups. The query pattern stays "sum buckets." > 💬 "Breakdowns are just extra dimensions in the bucket key — same sum-the-buckets pattern, more storage." --- 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a write-heavy, read-light view-count service. Half a million view events a second come in, hit a stateless ingest API that appends them to Kafka keyed by video_id and returns 202 immediately — so the database can never reject a viewer. Aggregation workers consume in batches, dedup on a client event_id, and increment two things: an all-time counter and a per-hour time bucket. The creator dashboard reads from Redis, and on a miss sums the trailing 168 hourly buckets for the week and 720 for the month — so I store fine buckets once and derive any window. The hard parts are hot videos, which I solve by splitting a counter into N sub-counters and summing on read, and accuracy, which is at-least-once-plus-dedup giving approximate counts within a tenth of a percent. Counts are fully reconstructable by replaying Kafka, so my RPO is near zero. I deliberately chose approximate, eventually-consistent, hour-granular counts — and I'd redesign the dedup and freshness paths if this had to be billing-grade or real-time."

High-Level Design: Design Video's View Count Service for YouTube/Netflix/Instagram

#287 · Apr 2025
● offer
High Scalability
High Availability
High-Level Design (HLD)
System Design
Backend

▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Design a system which can give total, monthly weekly views on a video to show on creator's dashboard.

transcript.thinking: I'm going to write out the full system design for a video view count service, starting with the conceptual overview and then working through the capacity calculations. I'm ready to rewrite the next thinking. Since my current rewritten thinking is empty, I'll start fresh with whatever comes next.

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — counting accuracy, the write firehose, and the time-window rollups — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension here: **a massive, bursty write firehose of view events** (hundreds of thousands per second) versus a **creator dashboard that only needs approximate, time-windowed totals read at human speed**. We can trade exactness and freshness for throughput — and that trade is the whole design.

🏢 I'll target a **Meta/YouTube-style bar** — front-load scale and the write hot-path. Tell me if it's Amazon (I'll make cost/ops first-class) and I'll re-tilt.

**Functional (core flows — above the line):**
- Users (viewers) generate a **view event** when they watch a video — the system must ingest and count it.
- Creators should be able to see **total views** on a video.
- Creators should be able to see **windowed views** — last 7 days (weekly) and last 30 days (monthly), per video, on their dashboard.

**Below the line (out of scope):**
- Defining *what counts as a view* (30s watched? Netflix 2-min rule?) — I'll treat "view" as a validated event upstream.
- Per-viewer analytics, demographics, watch-time, retention curves.
- Real-time sub-second counters shown to viewers ("1.2M views" under the player) — I'll mention it but the *creator dashboard* is the ask.
- Monetization / ad counting (needs exactly-once, different system).

**Non-Functional (talkable targets):**
- **Scale:** ~500K view events/sec peak ingest; billions of videos; dashboard reads ~thousands/sec.
- **Availability:** 4 nines (99.99%) on the read dashboard; ingest must never drop on the floor — degrade to buffering, not data loss.
- **Latency:** dashboard p99 < ~300ms; ingest accept p99 < ~50ms (fire-and-forget).
- **Consistency:** **eventual** is fine — counts can lag seconds-to-a-minute. Approximate counts (±0.1%) are acceptable for the dashboard; I am NOT building exactly-once accounting here.
- **Durability:** an accepted event should survive a node crash (RPO near zero via a durable log), but a few dropped events under catastrophe is tolerable for *view counts* (not for billing).

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** true exactly-once counting is expensive and unnecessary for view counts; I'll deliberately choose at-least-once + idempotent dedup and accept a tiny error bound. If this were ad-billing I'd redesign.

> 💬 "Before I draw anything — the key insight is the read and write profiles are wildly different: a firehose in, gentle dashboard queries out. So I'll optimize the two paths separately."

🎙️ **Script:** "So this is a view-count service feeding a creator dashboard — total, weekly, and monthly views per video. The thing that jumps out at me is the asymmetry: we're ingesting maybe half a million view events a second, but the dashboard is read by creators a few thousand times a second and they're happy with numbers that are a few seconds stale and approximately right. That means I'll treat counts as eventually consistent and approximate, optimize the write path for raw throughput with a durable buffer so we never lose accepted events, and pre-aggregate on the read side so the dashboard is just a cheap key lookup. I'm explicitly not building exactly-once billing-grade accounting — I'll call that out as the line I'm not crossing."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each one moves the design:

- **"Is approximate good enough, or do these counts drive payments?"** — This is the biggest fork. Approximate → counters/sketches and we're done. Exact → exactly-once pipeline, dedup at scale, much more cost. *I'll assume approximate.*
- **"What windows exactly — rolling 7/30 days, or calendar week/month?"** — Rolling windows need time-bucketed counts I can sum; calendar windows are simpler. *I'll assume rolling 7-day and 30-day, plus all-time total.*
- **"How fresh must the dashboard be?"** — Sub-second changes the architecture vs. "within a minute is fine." *I'll assume ~1 minute freshness is acceptable.*
- **"Do we need per-region breakdowns or one global number?"** — Multi-region rollup adds a merge step. *I'll assume one global count per video, but ingest is multi-region.*
- **"What's the dedup requirement — does a viewer refreshing count once or many times?"** — Defines idempotency key. *I'll assume each validated view event arrives with a unique event_id and we dedup on it (at-least-once delivery).*
- **"Retention of raw events?"** — Drives storage. *I'll assume raw events kept ~90 days in cheap storage, aggregates kept forever.*

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — approximate counts, rolling 7/30-day windows, ~1-minute freshness, dedup on a client event_id. Are you happy with me treating this as approximate and eventually consistent, or do you want me to design the exact/billing version instead?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll defer full estimation and just compute the few numbers that actually drive decisions: the write QPS, the durable-log throughput, and the aggregate storage.

Let me compute these exactly.

blocks.name: run_python

input.content:
# Assumptions
peak_views_per_sec = 500_000          # peak ingest
avg_views_per_sec  = 150_000          # average
event_bytes        = 200              # video_id, event_id, ts, region, device

# Write firehose
print("Peak ingest QPS:", peak_views_per_sec)
print("Avg ingest QPS :", avg_views_per_sec)

# Durable log bandwidth at peak
log_bw_mb = peak_views_per_sec * event_bytes / 1e6
print(f"Peak log write bandwidth: {log_bw_mb:.0f} MB/s")

# Daily event volume
daily = avg_views_per_sec * 86400
print(f"Daily events: {daily/1e9:.1f} billion/day")

# Raw event storage for 90 days, replication factor 3
raw_bytes = daily * 90 * event_bytes * 3
print(f"Raw events 90d x3 replicas: {raw_bytes/1e12:.0f} TB")

# Aggregate store: per-video time buckets.
# Say 2 billion videos, but only ~50M get views in any given hour.
# All-time + hourly buckets for 30 days (720 buckets) per active video.
videos_total = 2_000_000_000
agg_row_bytes = 40   # video_id + bucket + count
# all-time counter per video:
alltime = videos_total * agg_row_bytes * 3
print(f"All-time counters (2B videos) x3: {alltime/1e12:.1f} TB")

# hourly buckets: assume 200M videos viewed in trailing 30d, 720 buckets
hourly = 200_000_000 * 720 * agg_row_bytes * 3
print(f"Hourly buckets (200M active x720) x3: {hourly/1e12:.0f} TB")

# Redis hot counter ceiling check
redis_node = 150_000   # ops/sec realistic
print(f"Redis nodes needed for peak if all hit one tier: {peak_views_per_sec/redis_node:.1f}")

log_bw_mb = peak_views_per_sec * event_bytes / 1e6
print(f"Peak log write bandwidth: {log_bw_mb:.0f} MB/s")

daily = avg_views_per_sec * 86400
print(f"Daily events: {daily/1e9:.1f} billion/day")

raw_bytes = daily * 90 * event_bytes * 3
print(f"Raw events 90d x3 replicas: {raw_bytes/1e12:.0f} TB")

videos_total = 2_000_000_000
agg_row_bytes = 40
alltime = videos_total * agg_row_bytes * 3
print(f"All-time counters (2B videos) x3: {alltime/1e12:.1f} TB")

hourly = 200_000_000 * 720 * agg_row_bytes * 3
print(f"Hourly buckets (200M active x720) x3: {hourly/1e12:.0f} TB")

blocks.md:
| Metric | Value | Why it matters | |---|---|---| | Peak ingest | ~500K events/s | The binding constraint — drives the durable log + partitioning | | Avg ingest | ~150K events/s | Sizes steady-state aggregation workers | | Log write bandwidth | ~100 MB/s | Trivial for Kafka — buffering is not the bottleneck, processing is | | Daily events | ~13 billion/day | Sizes raw retention | | Raw events (90d, x3) | ~700 TB | Cheap object/columnar storage, not the primary DB | | All-time counters (2B videos, x3) | ~0.2 TB | Tiny — fits a KV store easily | | Hourly buckets (200M active x720, x3) | ~17 TB | The real aggregate store; drives the window queries | | Redis nodes for peak | ~3-4 | Only if every event hit one counter tier | **The ONE number that forces a decision:** ~500K writes/sec. **Flip threshold:** a single Redis node tops out ~150K ops/s and a single Kafka partition ~tens of MB/s — so we **cannot** have one counter or one partition; we **must** shard the ingest and pre-aggregate, never letting raw event QPS hit the database. Glossary as I go: **QPS** *(queries per second — how many requests hit the system each second)*. **Kafka** *(a durable append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a downstream consumer is slow — here it absorbs the 500K/s spike)*. **TTL** *(time-to-live — auto-expiry on cached data)*. 🎙️ **Script:** "Let me get the scale on the board. We're looking at about half a million view events a second at peak, versus a dashboard that's read maybe a few thousand times a second — so it's a write-heavy, read-light system with a huge asymmetry. That firehose is only about 100 megabytes a second of raw bytes, so *buffering* it is easy — Kafka eats that for breakfast. The hard part is that no single counter can take 500K writes a second, so the whole game is sharding the ingest and pre-aggregating into time buckets, so by the time a creator loads their dashboard it's just a cheap key lookup. Storage-wise the aggregates are small — a few terabytes — and the 700 terabytes of raw events go to cheap columnar storage we rarely touch." ## 4. Core Entities First draft of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later: - **View Event** — a single validated watch: which video, a unique event_id (for dedup), timestamp, region, device. - **Video** — the thing being counted; identified by video_id (our partition key everywhere). - **Counter / Aggregate** — the pre-computed numbers: all-time total, and per-time-bucket counts that we sum into 7-day and 30-day windows. - **Creator** — owns videos; the dashboard caller we authorize against. 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are simple: a view event coming in, the video it belongs to, the creator who owns it, and the aggregates — the all-time counter plus time-bucketed counts we roll up into weekly and monthly windows. This is just a first pass; I'll nail down the exact fields when I get to the data model." ## 5. API / Interface Going one-by-one through the functional requirements: **Ingest a view (FR1):** ``` POST /v1/views Body: { video_id, event_id, ts, region, device } Resp: 202 Accepted ``` Why: POST creates an event; **202** (not 200) because we accept-and-buffer — processing is async/fire-and-forget so ingest p99 stays under ~50ms. `event_id` is the **idempotency key** — a retried client send dedups to the same logical view. The view's *validity* (did they watch long enough) is decided upstream; we trust the event. **Get total + windows for a video (FR2, FR3):** ``` GET /v1/videos/{video_id}/views?windows=total,7d,30d Resp: 200 { video_id, total, "7d": ..., "30d": ..., as_of_ts } ``` Why: GET reads; one endpoint returns all windows so the dashboard makes one call. `as_of_ts` tells the creator how fresh the number is (honest about eventual consistency). **Get all of a creator's videos (dashboard load):** ``` GET /v1/creators/me/videos/views?windows=total,7d,30d Resp: 200 [ {video_id, total, 7d, 30d}, ... ] ``` Why: dashboard shows many videos; batch read avoids N calls. **Security:** caller identity comes from the **JWT** *(a signed token proving who you are, so we never trust a client-supplied creator_id)*. We authorize that the creator owns the requested videos before returning counts. Ingest is rate-limited per-IP/per-device to blunt bot inflation. 🎙️ **Script:** "Three endpoints. Ingest is a POST that returns 202 Accepted — we buffer and process async, and the client-supplied event_id is our idempotency key so retries don't double-count. Reads are GETs: one returns total, 7-day and 30-day for a single video in one shot with an as-of timestamp so we're honest about freshness, and a batch one returns every video for the logged-in creator's dashboard. Identity always comes from the JWT — I never trust a client-sent creator_id." ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) ### 6.1 "Ingest and count a view event" Start minimal: accept events without dropping them, and don't let the firehose touch a database directly. New components: a stateless **Ingest API**, a **Kafka** durable log partitioned by video_id, and **aggregation workers** that consume and increment counters in a **counter store**. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients P[Player clients] end subgraph Ingest_write_path LB[Load balancer] ING[Ingest API stateless] K[[Kafka log
partitioned by video_id]] AGG[Aggregation workers] CS[(Counter store
sharded KV)] end P -->|1 POST view 202| LB LB -->|2 forward| ING ING -->|3 append event| K K -->|4 consume batches| AGG AGG -->|5 dedup + increment| CS classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class LB,ING,K,AGG,CS cold; ``` 1. Player fires a view event; **Load balancer** routes to any **Ingest API** node. 2. LB forwards to a stateless ingest node (scales horizontally — no state to coordinate). 3. Ingest validates shape, then **appends the event to Kafka**, keyed by video_id so all events for a video land on one partition (ordering + locality). Returns 202 immediately. *State change: event now durably buffered.* 4. **Aggregation workers** consume Kafka in batches (e.g., flush every 1s or 10K events). 5. Each worker **dedups on event_id** (recent-window set) and **increments the counter store**. *State change: counts updated.* > 💬 "I'm deliberately putting Kafka between ingest and the database. Ingest just appends and returns — so a database slowdown can never reject a viewer's event; it just queues up." ### 6.2 "Show total views" + 6.3 "Show weekly/monthly views" Now the read side. The trick: workers don't keep just one number — they increment **per-time-bucket counters** (e.g., hourly buckets) AND an all-time total. Windows = sum of buckets. New components: a **Read API**, a **Redis cache** for hot dashboard reads, and the **bucketed aggregate store**. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients P[Player clients] D[Creator dashboard] end subgraph Write_path LB[Load balancer] ING[Ingest API] K[[Kafka log]] AGG[Aggregation workers] end subgraph Read_path RAPI[Read API] RED[Redis cache] CS[(Aggregate store
all-time + hourly buckets
sharded by video_id)] end P -->|1 POST view| LB --> ING -->|2 append| K K -->|3 consume| AGG -->|4 increment bucket + total| CS D -->|5 GET views| RAPI RAPI -->|6 check cache| RED RED -->|7 miss| CS RAPI -->|8 sum buckets to 7d 30d| D classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class LB,ING,K,AGG,CS cold; class RAPI,RED,D hot; ``` 5. Creator dashboard calls **Read API**. 6. Read API checks **Redis** *(in-memory cache — sub-ms lookups, holds recently-viewed videos' counts with a ~1-min TTL)*. 7. On miss, read the all-time counter and the relevant hourly buckets from the **aggregate store**. 8. **Sum the trailing 168 hourly buckets for 7d, 720 for 30d**, return with as_of_ts; populate Redis. > 💬 "Weekly and monthly aren't separate counters — they're just sums over hourly buckets. That's the key idea: store fine-grained buckets once, derive any window by summing." **Final (high-level):** the cumulative diagram above is the whole system — write path (amber) feeds the aggregate store; read path (green) serves the dashboard from cache + bucket sums. Load-bearing schema: aggregate store keyed by `(video_id, bucket_hour)` plus a `(video_id, total)` row. 🎙️ **Script:** "End to end: a view comes in, the ingest API appends it to Kafka keyed by video_id and returns immediately. Aggregation workers consume in batches, dedup on event_id, and bump two things — the all-time total and the current hour's bucket. On the read side, the dashboard hits the read API, which serves from Redis if it's warm, and on a miss reads the all-time counter plus the trailing hourly buckets and sums 168 of them for the week and 720 for the month. The big idea is I store fine-grained hourly buckets once and derive every window by summing — I never keep a separate 'weekly counter' that I'd have to expire." 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The two juiciest parts are how I make counting accurate-enough at 500K/s without a hot-shard meltdown, and how I do the windowed rollups efficiently. Which do you want first — or is there something else you'd probe?" ## 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Key fields | Store + why | |---|---|---| | **View Event (raw)** | event_id, video_id, ts, region, device | **Kafka** (transient buffer) → **columnar object store** (S3/Parquet) for 90d. Append-only, rarely read, cheap. | | **All-time counter** | video_id (PK), count | **Sharded KV** (e.g., Cassandra/ScyllaDB or Redis-backed) — point read/write by video_id. | | **Hourly bucket** | (video_id, bucket_hour) PK, count | **Wide-column store** (Cassandra) — partition by video_id, clustered by bucket_hour → range-scan trailing N hours fast. | | **Dedup set** | event_id → seen (TTL ~10 min) | **Redis** with TTL — short-window idempotency. | | **Video → creator** | video_id, creator_id | **SQL** (metadata, low volume) for ownership authz. | **Partition/shard key is video_id everywhere** — co-locates all of a video's events and counts, makes window scans a single-partition range read. **Consistency:** counters are eventually consistent (last-writer + atomic increment); ownership lookup is strongly consistent (it's authz). **Cassandra** *(a wide-column NoSQL store that shards by partition key and handles massive write throughput with tunable consistency — here it holds the bucketed counts because we need fast writes and partition-local range scans)*. 🎙️ **Script:** "Everything is keyed by video_id so a video's events and counts live together. Raw events go to Kafka then cheap columnar storage for 90 days. The counts live in a wide-column store like Cassandra — all-time as a single counter row, and hourly buckets clustered by hour so reading a 30-day window is one partition-local range scan of 720 small rows. Dedup uses a short-TTL Redis set on event_id. The only strongly-consistent thing is the video-to-creator ownership lookup, because that's authorization." ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to "firehose in, gentle reads out" and ask which path the bottleneck is on — that re-derives every answer here. ### How do we count 500K events/sec without a hot shard melting down? #### Bad: Increment a row in a SQL DB per event **Approach:** each view does `UPDATE counts SET c=c+1 WHERE video_id=?`. **Challenges:** 500K writes/sec of row-locked updates is impossible on one row; a viral video sends every write to ONE row → lock contention, the **hot-shard** problem. ⚠️ **Trap:** candidates who write straight to the DB per event fail here — you must decouple and batch. #### Good: Kafka buffer + batched increments **Approach:** append to Kafka (keyed by video_id), workers consume in micro-batches and do one increment per video per batch (combine 10K events into "+10K"). **Challenges:** dramatically cuts DB write QPS, but a **celebrity video** still pins all its events to one Kafka partition and one worker → single-partition bottleneck. #### Great: Kafka + in-memory pre-aggregation + hot-key splitting **Approach:** Workers keep an in-memory map and flush aggregated deltas every ~1s (so the DB sees ~one write per active video per second, not per event). For **hot keys**, split the counter into N sub-counters: write to `video_id#shard_k` (k = random 0..N-1), and **sum the N shards on read**. This spreads a celebrity's writes across N partitions/rows. ```mermaid flowchart TD K[[Kafka by video_id]] -->|consume batch| W[Worker in-memory aggregate] W -->|flush delta 1s| SUB[(Sub-counters
video_id#0..N)] R[Read API] -->|sum N shards| SUB classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class K,W,SUB cold; class R hot; ``` **Math:** without batching, 500K writes/s hits the DB. Batching 10K events into 1 write per video per second drops DB writes to roughly the number of *active videos/sec* — say ~50K/s spread across shards → comfortably ~10K/s per Cassandra node. Hot-key splitting with N=10 keeps any single celebrity under a node's ~150K/s ceiling. 🎙️ **Script:** "The naive thing — incrementing a DB row per view — dies instantly because a viral video hammers one row. So I buffer everything in Kafka and have workers aggregate in memory and flush a combined delta about once a second, which turns 500K writes a second into one write per active video. For the celebrity case where even one video's stream is too hot, I split its counter into ten sub-counters with a random suffix and sum them on read — that fans the writes across ten partitions so no single row is a bottleneck." 🧠 **If they ask "why not just one counter per video?":** "Because a single viral video's writes all serialize on one row or partition — the hot-shard problem. Splitting into N sub-counters trades a slightly more expensive read, summing N rows, for write parallelism, which is the right trade since writes are the firehose." ### How do we compute rolling 7-day and 30-day windows efficiently? #### Bad: Scan raw events at read time **Approach:** on dashboard load, count events in the last 7/30 days from raw storage. **Challenges:** scanning billions of rows per dashboard load — seconds-to-minutes latency, blows the 300ms SLO. #### Great: Pre-bucketed time-series counters **Approach:** workers increment a **per-hour bucket** `(video_id, hour)`. A 7-day window = sum of trailing 168 hourly buckets; 30-day = 720. Range-scan is partition-local and tiny. Old buckets expire via TTL (keep ~35 days). The all-time total is its own monotonic counter (never expires). ```mermaid flowchart LR E[Event ts] -->|floor to hour| B[(video_id, hour bucket)] Q[7d query] -->|sum last 168| B Q2[30d query] -->|sum last 720| B classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class Q,Q2 hot; ``` **Trade-off:** hourly granularity means "last 7 days" is accurate to the hour, not the second — totally fine for a dashboard. If they want minute precision, smaller buckets cost ~60x storage. Pre-compute daily rollups to make 30d even cheaper (sum 30 daily + a few hourly for the current day). 🎙️ **Script:** "I never scan raw events at read time — that's death. Instead workers write per-hour buckets, and a weekly number is just the sum of the last 168 hourly buckets, monthly is 720. It's a tiny partition-local range scan. Buckets expire after about 35 days, and the all-time total is a separate counter that never expires. The cost is hour-level precision on the windows, which is completely fine for a creator dashboard." 🧠 **If they ask "why hourly, not store every window?":** "Storing fine buckets once and summing lets me serve *any* window — 7d, 30d, even custom ranges — without maintaining and expiring separate weekly/monthly counters that drift. One source of truth." ### How do we avoid double-counting (idempotency)? #### Great: at-least-once delivery + event_id dedup **Approach:** Kafka gives at-least-once, so retries/redelivery can duplicate. Each event carries a client-generated **event_id**; workers check a Redis dedup set (TTL ~10 min, covering the redelivery window) before incrementing. Within a worker's batch, dedup in-memory too. **Trade-off:** the TTL window bounds dedup — a duplicate arriving hours later (rare) slips through, causing a tiny over-count. Acceptable given ±0.1% tolerance. For exact counting we'd need a persistent dedup store keyed by event_id, much costlier. 🎙️ **Script:** "Kafka is at-least-once so I have to dedup. Every event has a unique event_id and workers check a short-TTL Redis set before counting it. The TTL covers the realistic redelivery window; a duplicate arriving days later would slip through, but that's a fraction of a percent and within tolerance. If this were billing I'd use a persistent dedup store and eat the cost." 🧠 **If they ask "what about bot/fraud inflation?":** "That's upstream view-validation's job — rate limiting, bot detection, watch-time thresholds decide what's a valid event before it reaches me. I just count validated events idempotently." ### How do we approximate cheaply when even counters are too much? (optional/scale) For *unique* viewer counts (not in scope but a likely probe) I'd use **HyperLogLog** *(a probabilistic sketch that estimates distinct counts in ~12KB with ~2% error instead of storing every viewer id)* — Redis supports it natively. For total views, exact counters are cheap enough so I keep them. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost - **Availability:** read path 4 nines via multi-AZ Redis + Cassandra replicas; ingest path stays up as long as Kafka does (replicated 3x). - **Per-dependency degradation:** - *Cassandra slow/down:* ingest unaffected (Kafka buffers); reads serve stale Redis values with an older as_of_ts. Workers replay from Kafka offset when DB recovers — no data loss. - *Redis down:* reads fall back to direct Cassandra (higher latency, still correct); dedup falls back to in-batch only (tiny over-count risk). - *Worker crash:* Kafka consumer group rebalances; offsets are committed only after DB flush → at-least-once, no loss. - *Kafka down:* ingest API buffers locally briefly then 503s — the only true degradation; mitigated by 3x replication + multi-broker. - **RPO/RTO:** Kafka RPO≈0 (replicated). Aggregate store RPO ~minutes (replayable from Kafka/raw events — counts are *reconstructable*, a big advantage). RTO minutes via replica failover. **Tested restore:** periodically rebuild a sample video's counts from raw events and reconcile against live counters (drift alarm). - **Multi-region:** ingest regionally, each region writes local sub-counters; a rollup job merges per-region counts into the global number (eventually consistent). Single global write region not needed since increments are commutative. - **Cost (rough monthly):** dominant driver is **Kafka + worker compute** for the firehose and **~700TB raw storage** (cheap on object storage, ~$15K/mo). Aggregate store is small. Ballpark mid-five-figures/month; raw event storage and Kafka throughput dominate, not the dashboard reads. - **Observability:** SLO burn-rate alerts on ingest accept rate and read p99; consumer lag alert on Kafka (the leading indicator of staleness); a daily reconciliation job comparing summed buckets vs raw counts. 🎙️ **Script:** "The beautiful property here is counts are reconstructable — if the aggregate store gets corrupted I replay Kafka or the raw events and rebuild them, so my real RPO is near zero. The only hard-down failure is Kafka itself, which I replicate three ways. Everything else degrades gracefully: DB slow just means staler dashboard numbers, Redis down just means slower reads. My number-one health metric is Kafka consumer lag, because that's exactly how stale the dashboard is." ## 10. Trade-off Ledger - **Approximate (±0.1%) vs exact counts** — I gave up exactness (TTL-bounded dedup, hot-key shard summing) to hit 500K/s cheaply. *Reverses if* counts drive payments → persistent dedup + exactly-once → far higher cost. - **Hourly bucket granularity** — gave up sub-hour window precision for ~60x storage savings. *Reverses if* product needs minute-level "views in last hour" trends. - **Eventual consistency / ~1-min freshness** — gave up read-your-own-writes immediacy (a creator's brand-new view may not show for up to a minute due to Kafka lag + flush interval). *Reverses if* sub-second live counts are required → shorter flush + dual-write to a live counter, more load. 🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'd flag: I chose approximate over exact, hourly buckets over finer granularity, and eventual over strong consistency. All three trade accuracy or freshness for throughput and cost, which is right for a creator dashboard. Every one of them flips if the requirement changes to billing-grade or real-time — and I'd redesign the dedup and flush paths specifically." ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **1. What if a video goes viral — the hot-key problem?** All its events hash to one partition/row and overwhelm it. I split the counter into N sub-counters with a random suffix per write and sum on read, spreading writes across N partitions. I can even detect hotness dynamically and raise N for that video. The cost is an N-way read, negligible since reads are rare. > 💬 "I shard the hot video's counter into N sub-counters and sum them on read — writes fan out, reads pay a tiny tax." **2. How do you prevent double-counting on Kafka redelivery?** Each event has a unique event_id; workers dedup against a short-TTL Redis set before incrementing, plus in-batch dedup. Offsets commit only after a successful DB flush, so it's at-least-once with idempotent application. The TTL bounds the dedup window, allowing a negligible over-count for very-late duplicates. > 💬 "At-least-once delivery plus event_id dedup makes the increment effectively idempotent within the redelivery window." **3. How fresh is the dashboard, and why isn't it real-time?** Freshness equals Kafka consumer lag plus the worker flush interval — about a minute normally. I trade immediacy for batching efficiency. If sub-second is required, I'd shorten the flush and keep a separate live counter, accepting more DB load. > 💬 "Freshness is basically consumer lag plus flush interval — about a minute, tunable down at a cost." **4. How do you compute rolling windows without scanning raw events?** I pre-aggregate into hourly buckets keyed by (video_id, hour); 7-day is the sum of 168 buckets, 30-day is 720, a tiny partition-local range scan. Daily rollups make 30d cheaper still. Old buckets TTL out after ~35 days. > 💬 "Windows are sums of pre-computed hourly buckets — never a raw scan." **5. What happens when the aggregate DB goes down?** Ingest is unaffected because Kafka keeps buffering; reads serve stale cached values with an older as_of_ts. When the DB recovers, workers resume from committed offsets and catch up. Because counts are reconstructable from Kafka/raw events, there's effectively no permanent loss. > 💬 "DB down just means staler reads — ingest keeps buffering and we replay on recovery." **6. How do you handle multi-region ingest?** Each region counts locally into region-scoped sub-counters, and a rollup merges them into the global number. Since increments are commutative and we want eventual consistency, no single global write region is needed. Cross-region merge adds seconds of lag, fine for the dashboard. > 💬 "Regions count locally and we merge — increments commute, so no global coordinator needed." **7. Is there any single global coordinator / bottleneck?** No central sequencer — that's deliberate. The closest is a per-video counter, which I shard for hot keys. Kafka partitions and Cassandra shards both scale horizontally by video_id, so there's no single point that caps throughput. > 💬 "I avoided any global counter or sequencer — everything shards by video_id." **8. How do you stop bots/fraud inflating counts?** View *validity* is decided upstream — rate limiting, device fingerprinting, watch-time thresholds — before events reach me. I only count validated events and dedup them. Keeping fraud logic upstream keeps my pipeline a pure, fast counter. > 💬 "Fraud filtering is upstream; I just idempotently count what's already validated." **9. Why Kafka and not just write to the DB or use a queue like SQS?** Kafka gives a durable, replayable, partitioned log — replay is what makes counts reconstructable and lets me rebuild state after failures. A plain queue loses messages once consumed; the DB can't absorb 500K/s of row updates. The log is the backbone of both durability and recovery. > 💬 "Kafka's replayability is what gives me near-zero RPO and rebuildable counters." **10. How do you size and scale the workers?** Workers are a Kafka consumer group; partition count sets max parallelism, and I scale workers up to the partition count. I monitor consumer lag as the scaling signal — lag rising means add workers or partitions. Each worker does cheap in-memory aggregation, so a handful per region handles 150K/s average. > 💬 "Workers scale with Kafka partitions; consumer lag is my autoscaling trigger." **11. What's the storage cost driver?** Not the counters — those are a couple terabytes. It's the ~700TB of 90-day raw events, which I push to cheap columnar object storage, and Kafka retention. I keep raw events mainly for reconciliation and reprocessing, and could shorten retention to cut cost. > 💬 "Counters are tiny; raw event retention dominates storage, so it lives on cheap object storage." **12. How would you extend this to per-country or per-device breakdowns later?** Add region/device to the bucket key — `(video_id, hour, region)` — and sum the appropriate subset. Storage grows by the cardinality of the new dimension, so I'd only add dimensions the product needs and pre-aggregate the common rollups. The query pattern stays "sum buckets." > 💬 "Breakdowns are just extra dimensions in the bucket key — same sum-the-buckets pattern, more storage." --- 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a write-heavy, read-light view-count service. Half a million view events a second come in, hit a stateless ingest API that appends them to Kafka keyed by video_id and returns 202 immediately — so the database can never reject a viewer. Aggregation workers consume in batches, dedup on a client event_id, and increment two things: an all-time counter and a per-hour time bucket. The creator dashboard reads from Redis, and on a miss sums the trailing 168 hourly buckets for the week and 720 for the month — so I store fine buckets once and derive any window. The hard parts are hot videos, which I solve by splitting a counter into N sub-counters and summing on read, and accuracy, which is at-least-once-plus-dedup giving approximate counts within a tenth of a percent. Counts are fully reconstructable by replaying Kafka, so my RPO is near zero. I deliberately chose approximate, eventually-consistent, hour-granular counts — and I'd redesign the dedup and freshness paths if this had to be billing-grade or real-time."
